perturbation.original_text: Business Day means a day other than a Saturday, Sunday or bank or public holiday in England or Wales;
perturbation.changed_text: Business Day means any day of the week, including Saturdays, Sundays, and any days recognized as bank or public holidays in England or Wales;
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of "Business Day" excludes weekends and public holidays. The modified definition includes them, creating a legal contradiction by expanding the standard understanding of what constitutes a business day.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Bills of Exchange Act 1882 Section 92(1) states 'Where the time limited for doing any act or thing is less than three days, in reckoning time non-business days are excluded.' By including weekends and holidays in the definition of 'Business Day', the modified contract text contradicts the implied exclusion of these days within the Bills of Exchange Act, creating potential confusion in contract enforcement and interpretation, especially regarding deadlines related to financial instruments.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Bills of Exchange Act 1882 - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia Act of Parliament of the United Kingdom United Kingdom legislation Bills of Exchange Act 1882 [ a ] Act of Parliament Parliament of the United Kingdom Long title An Act to codify the law relating to Bills of Exchange, Cheques, and Promissory Notes. Citation 45 & 46 Vict. c. 61 Introduced by Sir John Lubbock MP ( Commons ) Territorial extent United Kingdom Dates Royal assent 18 August 1882 Commencement 18 August 1882 [ b ] Other legislation Amends See § Repealed enactments Repeals/revokes See § Repealed enactments Amended by Statute Law Revision Act 1898 Bills of Exchange (Time of Noting) Act 1917 Finance Act 1939 Cheques Act 1957 National Loans Act 1968 Decimal Currency Act 1969 Finance Act (Northern Ireland) 1970 Finance Act 1970 Banking and Financial Dealings Act 1971 Consumer Credit Act 1974 Administration of Justice Act 1977 Judicature (Northern Ireland) Act 1978 Law Reform (Miscellaneous Provisions) (Scotland) Act 1985 Cheques Act 1992 Status: Amended History of passage through Parliament Text of statute as originally enacted Revised text of statute as amended Text of the Bills of Exchange Act 1882 as in force today (including any amendments) within the United Kingdom, from legislation.gov.uk . The Bills of Exchange Act 1882 ( 45 & 46 Vict. c. 61) is an act of the Parliament of the United Kingdom that codified the law relating to bills of exchange . Bills of exchange are widely used to finance trade and, when discounted with a financial institution, to obtain credit. The act was drafted by Sir Mackenzie Chalmers , who later drafted the Sale of Goods Act 1893 ( 56 & 57 Vict. c. 71) and the Marine Insurance Act 1906 ( 8 Edw. 7 . c. 41). [ 1 ] Passage [ edit ] Leave to bring in the Bills of Exchange Bill to the House of Commons was granted to Sir John Lubbock MP , Arthur Cohen MP , Lewis Fry MP , Sir Ambrose Hardinge Giffard MP and Charles James Monk MP on 15 February 1882. perturbation.justification: The modified contract text redefines "Business Day" to include weekends and holidays, directly conflicting with the Bills of Exchange Act 1882, which, as the Law Explanation states and Snippet 2 describes, governs deadlines for financial instruments. The Law Explanation argues that this change contradicts the implied exclusion of non-business days, as supported by the Bills of Exchange Act 1882. While Snippet 1 doesn't directly state the exclusion of non-business days, Snippet 2 describes the Act as codifying laws related to bills of exchange, implying a specific legal context where the definition of "Business Day" is relevant. This redefinition creates a legal contradiction because it deviates from the standard understanding and potential legal implications of 'Business Day' within the context of financial deadlines governed by the Act, potentially leading to misinterpretations and enforcement issues. This aligns with the Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction type.

perturbation.original_text: Intellectual Property Rights means intellectual and industrial property rights, including copyright (including moral rights), patents, know-how, trade secrets, trademarks, service marks, trade names, design rights, registered designs, get-up, database rights, chip topography rights, mask works, utility models, domain names, rights in trade and business names and all similar rights and, in each case: (a) whether registered or not; (b) including any applications to protect or register such rights; (c) including all renewals and extensions of such rights or applications; (d) whether vested, contingent or future; (e) including rights in the nature of unfair competition rights and rights to sue in passing off; and (f) wherever existing;
perturbation.changed_text: Intellectual Property Rights means intellectual and industrial property rights, specifically excluding copyright and moral rights, patents, and trade secrets. These rights are limited to registered trademarks only, applicable solely within England and Wales, regardless of registration status elsewhere.
perturbation.explanation: This modification significantly narrows the scope of "Intellectual Property Rights" by explicitly excluding several key categories like copyright, patents, and trade secrets. The original text encompasses a broader definition.
perturbation.law_citation: Copyright, Designs and Patents Act 1988, Section 1
perturbation.law_explanation: The Copyright, Designs and Patents Act 1988, Section 1, defines copyright as a property right that subsists in original literary, dramatic, musical, and artistic works. By excluding copyright and limiting intellectual property rights to registered trademarks applicable only within England and Wales, the modified contract contradicts the statutory definition and scope of intellectual property rights as defined under UK law, which includes automatic copyright protection.

perturbation.justification: The original contract text broadly defines Intellectual Property Rights, encompassing copyright, patents, trade secrets, and trademarks. The changed text significantly narrows this definition, specifically excluding copyright, patents, and trade secrets, limiting the rights to registered trademarks applicable only in England and Wales. The Law Explanation states that the Copyright, Designs and Patents Act 1988, Section 1, defines copyright as a property right. The changed text contradicts this law by excluding copyright from the definition of intellectual property rights. Snippet 1 shows that this act exists, but does not provide the text of Section 1 itself. The modification creates a significant legal contradiction because it alters the legally recognized scope of intellectual property rights, making the contract unenforceable as it deviates from statutory definitions.

perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information means any information in whatever form (whether in writing, electronic or digital form, verbally or by inspection of documents, computer systems or sites or pursuant to discussions or by any other means ) which is confidential in nature, designated orally or in writing by the Disclosers as confidential or which may reasonably be considered by a business person to be commercially sensitive provided by the Disclosers (directly or indirectly) by any means to the Recipient (or to any Authorised Person) including without limitation via its legal representatives before or after the date of this Agreement in connection with or in anticipation of the Purpose
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information means only information explicitly marked as 'Confidential' in writing by the Discloser and does not include any information that the Recipient independently develops or obtains from publicly available sources, regardless of its commercial sensitivity or nature.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition is broad, encompassing information confidential in nature or reasonably considered commercially sensitive. The modified definition is much narrower, requiring explicit written designation and excluding independently developed or publicly available information. This limits the scope of protection afforded to the Discloser.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Trade Secrets (Enforcement, etc.) Regulations 2018, Regulation 2(1) defines a trade secret as information that is secret, has commercial value because it is secret, and has been subject to reasonable steps to keep it secret.  The modified contract restricts 'Confidential Information' to only explicitly marked written information and disregards the reasonable steps to maintain secrecy. The modified text now contradicts the broader definition of a trade secret that doesn't require explicit written marking, as long as reasonable efforts were made to keep the information secret and it holds commercial value due to its secrecy.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract text narrows the definition of 'Confidential Information' to only explicitly marked written information, which contradicts the broader scope of trade secret protection as suggested by the Law Explanation. The Law Explanation refers to The Trade Secrets (Enforcement, etc.) Regulations 2018, which defines trade secrets more broadly, encompassing information that is secret, has commercial value because it is secret, and has been subject to reasonable steps to keep it secret, regardless of whether it is explicitly marked in writing. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is incomplete, it does reference 'The Trade Secrets (Enforcement, etc.) Regulations 2018', supporting the relevance of this law. The modified contract fails to align with the reasonable steps to maintain secrecy component, creating a legal contradiction based on misaligned terminology.

perturbation.original_text: Use means receive, store, transmit, access, read, analyse, disclose, share, print, copy, reproduce, extract, modify, adapt, incorporate, exploit or use Confidential Information in whole or in part in any manner whatsoever.
perturbation.changed_text: Use means only to receive, store, and read Confidential Information. All other actions, including analysis, disclosure, modification, and reproduction, are explicitly prohibited under all circumstances, regardless of Purpose.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of "Use" is broad, encompassing a wide range of actions related to Confidential Information, provided they are for the Purpose. The modified definition severely restricts the permissible actions, prohibiting many activities that would normally be considered legitimate uses of information.
perturbation.law_citation: Implied Terms in Contract Law (Various Case Laws)
perturbation.law_explanation: Although not a codified law, UK contract law recognizes implied terms, including the implied term of 'reasonable use' necessary for the effective performance of a contract. By restricting 'Use' to only receiving, storing, and reading, the modified contract text hinders the Recipient's ability to reasonably analyze or utilize the confidential information for the stated Purpose (Franchise agreement), contradicting the implied term of reasonable use necessary for fulfilling contractual obligations and potentially rendering the agreement commercially unworkable. This goes against the implied terms doctrine which allows for terms to be implied to give business efficacy to the contract.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract text significantly restricts the definition of 'Use' compared to the original. The Law Explanation argues that this restriction contradicts the implied term of 'reasonable use' necessary for the effective performance of the contract. This is because the original text's broad definition of "Use" allowed for a range of actions necessary to fulfill the purpose of the Franchise agreement. The modified version, by limiting "Use" to only receiving, storing, and reading, prevents the recipient from analyzing and utilizing the confidential information effectively. While the scraped snippets do not explicitly address UK contract law concerning implied terms, the Law Explanation's argument that this violates implied terms doctrine and the explanation that it hinders the ability to analyze the information suggests the creation of a legal contradiction according to the definition provided.
